results.title: Benchmarking Humans and VLMs for Autonomous Driving in Lima ...
results.content:
### Abstract

As Self-Driving Cars continue to be deployed in different cities around the world: how well will these systems generalize when exposed in new geographies? Moreover, how well will current multi-modal VLMs (Vision Language Models) be able to cognitively understand and act when faces with bizzare edge-case scenarios. In this talk I will aim to answer these questions through a Visual Question Answering (VQA) framework, where we show humans and VLMs a series of our own recorded dashcam footage from Lima and New York City and test for system divergence and convergence. Moreover we tests for these similarities/divergences in a factorial analysis with 3 groups: Humans from NYC, Humans from Lima and VLMs; and two first-person dashcam data recorded from both Lima and New York City. [...] Skip to yearly menu bar

results.title: Robusto2 Dataset: Humans vs AI in Lima & NYC Driving Scenarios | Artificio posted on the topic | LinkedIn
results.content:
📓 Last Saturday we had the fortune to present at Primary Venture Partners's HQ in New York City for their Humans vs AI #ReadingGroup about our upcoming dataset & research paper: #Robusto2, where we analyze the cross generalization capacity of #VLMs when prompted first person dashcam footage of humans driving in both #Lima and #NewYorkCity. Furthermore we collected human responses from participants from both Lima and New York City and showed them the same driving videos to assess the similarities and differences in responses to the same questions we asked the #VLMs under a Visual Question Answering framework. In this paper we answer in a highly controlled study: [...] 🤖🧑1) Are Humans from NYC more similar to VLMs than Humans of Lima in their pattern of responses across different driving scenarios?
🧠 2) How does answer similarity across Humans and VLMs depend on the types of questions we asked (Factual vs Ratings vs Counterfactual vs Reasoning)?
📐 3) Are some VLMs more biased than others in their answers?
🌎 4) Is the pattern of responses modulated by the type of dataset fed across all systems (i.e. will the similarity across systems change when prompted videos from Lima vs New York City?) [...] No alternative text description for this image

results.title: Peruvian startup Artificio looks to Lima's horrible traffic to solve one ...
results.content:
Arturo Deza, a former postdoctoral researcher at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology and a professor at Lima’s University of Engineering and Technology (UTEC), is on a mission to find out.

Professor Arturo Deza. Credit: Arturo Deza

His startup, Artificio, is developing a benchmarking platform specifically for autonomous driving in challenging conditions. The company is headquartered in Lima, which navigation app TomTom recently named as the city with the worst traffic in Latin America.

The challenge of autonomous driving

According to Deza, autonomous driving “has failed due to an overpromise that artificial vision systems were already solved, as if computer vision were fully developed.” [...] Additionally, Artificio proposes that the training of autonomous vehicle computer vision systems be conducted outside the city where they will be deployed. In machine learning, this is called Adversarial Training. This method trains systems with adverse and uncommon data, increasing the likelihood that neural networks will perform well in expected and unexpected situations.

Data collection on Peruvian streets [...] autonomous drivingstartups

Diego Lopez Marina: Diego Lopez Marina is a reporter for Peru Reports and Latin America Reports based in Lima. He also serves as Web Editor for ACI Prensa (the Spanish-language news outlet of EWTN News) and reported for El Comercio, Entrepreneur Magazine, El Nacional and others.
